items.title: Salary column with a data-entry typo
items.scenario: A HR dataset lists annual salaries mostly between $40k and $120k, but one row shows $4,000,000 because someone appended an extra zero. IQR fences flag it; after confirming with the source system, the team removes the row rather than clipping.
items.explanation: Extreme values that are clearly impossible for the domain are often errors. IQR catches them without assuming normality; removal is appropriate when the row count is large enough.

items.title: Fraud detection keeps outliers
items.scenario: Credit-card transaction amounts are right-skewed. Amounts 50× above a customer's typical spend are rare but are exactly the signal the classifier must learn. The team does not blanket-remove IQR outliers; they engineer features and use tree-based or robust models.
items.explanation: In anomaly and fraud settings, outliers are often the positive class. Blind removal would delete the most informative examples.

items.title: House prices before linear regression
items.scenario: A housing model uses square footage and age. Three mansions in a mostly middle-income zip code pull OLS coefficients. The team winsorizes price at the 99th percentile computed on the training fold, then fits regression on clipped targets.
items.explanation: Clipping limits leverage on the target while retaining rows. Pair with robust loss or Huber regression if extremes are valid but should not dominate.

items.title: Sensor readings with occasional spikes
items.scenario: Temperature sensors report 22–24 °C for hours, then a single 999 °C reading when the probe disconnects. Z-score on a rolling window flags the spike; the pipeline replaces it with NaN and imputes from neighboring timesteps.
items.explanation: Per-window or per-batch detection can separate valid drift from impossible spikes. Treatment may be imputation rather than row deletion for time series.
